subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermochemistry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
A student performs an experiment to determine the enthalpy of combustion of 2-propanol, C H OH(l), which
combusts in oxygen according to the equation above. The student heats a sample of water by burning some of
the C
H
OH(l) that is in an alcohol burner, as represented below. The alcohol burner uses a wick to draw liquid
up into the flame. The mass of C
H
OH(l) combusted is determined by weighing the alcohol burner before and
after combustion.
Data from the experiment are given in the table below.
Mass of C
H
OH(l) combusted 0.55 g
Mass of water heated 125.00 g
Initial temperature of water 22.0°C
Final temperature of water 51.1°C
Specific heat of water 4.18 J/(g·°C)

parts.prompt:
Calculate the magnitude of the heat energy, in kJ, absorbed by the water. (Assume that the energy released
from the combustion is completely transferred to the water.)

parts.prompt:
Based on the experimental data, if one mole of C
H
OH(l) is combusted, how much heat, in kJ, is released?
Report your answer with the correct number of significant figures.

parts.prompt:
A second student performs the experiment using the same mass of water at the same initial temperature.
However, the student uses an alcohol burner containing C
H
OH(l) that is contaminated with water, which
is miscible with C
H
OH(l). The difference in mass of the alcohol burner before and after the combustion in
this experiment is also 0.55 g. Would the final temperature of the water in the beaker heated by the
alcohol burner in this experiment be greater than, less than, or equal to the final temperature of the water
in the beaker in the first student’s experiment? Justify your answer.

scoring_points.description: State the correct answer with a valid chemical justification

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle
